turns.text: No. The -- if there were -- in this case, there was no request for a jury instruction. If there's not a request for a jury instruction and a jury finding venue, there is no need for it.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: But you could in a future case if you requested it. They just didn't request it.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan?

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Could you tell me, Mr. Yang, what the difference is between your theory and the Ninth Circuit's theory?

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: The Ninth Circuit, I think, isn't focusing as much on the -- kind of the doctrinal basis of having an intent and an effect in the district. I -- I think they were just looking at the statutory intent element but weren't fleshing it out. Ours is a refinement. We don't think our position is that inconsistent, at least in the context of this case, with the Ninth Circuit's. But they -- you know, they didn't have the same briefing as you have here in terms of fleshing out what the -- the significance of the statutory intent element and what that means for the nature of the crime that is committed when you're analyzing venue.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Okay. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch? Justice Kavanaugh?

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: 1512(i), your friend on the other side suggested that you think part of that might be unconstitutional. And I -- I didn't think that was correct, and I want to make sure that's not correct.

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: Yeah. No. There -- there is a question about whether 1512(i), certainly, the -- the prong that's relevant is -- and this is reproduced at page 32 of our brief. It's in the first paragraph towards this bottom third. Venue is proper in the district in which the official proceeding, whether or not pending or about to be instituted, was intended to be effected.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: Right? So that is an intent -- like, you have to intend the district to be effected, where -- where to proceed. So that is -- arguably, you could read that one of two ways. You could say all that's required is an intent. Your focus, you know it's happening in the Northern District of California, you intend it; that's enough. That's different than our position here. We would defend that that's --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Correct. I got that.

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: That -- we would -- we would -- we would --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: But, for 1512(i), that's enough, right?

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: And we would -- I'm -- I guess you get to make this call, but I -- I suspect we will defend that as a constitutional exercise of defining the crime. But you could also construe that. If you look at the legislative history, which we cite later down on the page, they talk specifically about killing a witness in Maryland, where the prosecution -- where there was a -- he was scheduled to testify in D.C.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Mm-hmm.

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: Right? And then it -- we don't quote this part, but it says, you know, they're providing jurisdiction where the obstruction is felt. Where the obstruction is felt, that evokes the question of whether actually the effect is required too. And so there's a -- there's an interpretive question. If you interpret it only to require intent, it's different. We still would defend it. But you could also require -- interpret it to be exactly what we're saying here, which is you intend and have an effect in the district.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And then, if you do not prevail in this case, obviously, one of the concerns in writing the opinion will be ripple effects. Here's your opportunity to tell us, in the event you don't prevail, what you're concerned about for the next case. You've covered a little of that with Justice Alito, I think.

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: Yeah. I -- I -- I think this really comes up a lot in these kind of inchoate crime types of theories, particularly in the context of obstructions of justice, attempted obstructions of justice. So Chapter 63, which you discussed at some length in Pugin, I think, the -- Congress has specifically addressed venue for purposes of 1503 and 1512 and 1512(i), but there are other provisions where there are inchoate attempt crimes there too. Our view is that because Congress was simply resolving a circuit conflict in the -- in the favor of the majority as clarifying where the offense was, that should be understood, you have to construe 1512 -- excuse me, 1519 in the corpus of that body of law and construe it similarly. Now it's possible you could construe it differently, and if that's the case, then our argument would not -- would not get us to home. But, if you were to simply construe this provision saying Congress hasn't spoken to the nature of the offense as it has done in 1512(i) with respect to 1503 and 1512, that would leave open the door for Congress to clarify, speak more clearly, with respect to where it thinks these inchoate --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: -- crimes take place.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett?

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Just a clarifying question that I hope is very simple. Did I understand you correctly to say that you don't think the intent to cause effects is enough to create venue in the place where the actus reus occurred?

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: Not on this statute because, unlike 1512(i), if you --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: But it would under 1512(i) --

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: If you construe --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: -- intent to cause effects is enough?

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: If you view 1512(i) as only requiring just the intent, not the effect, then we would say yes, that would be a -- that would be a sufficient basis for venue so that this -- so this -- this case has both constitutional questions and it has statutory questions, and the two are always intertwined because the question for venue is where is the crime committed, but the question of what the crime is requires understanding what Congress intended for the crime. Now the crime is -- we know this cannot be the case. The crime is more than the offense elements. We know that to be the case. We know that to be the case from conspiracy. Overt acts traditionally aren't --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: But conspiracy, Justice Gorsuch is right, conspiracy is a different kind of crime. I mean, it is continuing, and then you can be prosecuted for any of the acts within the scope of the conspiracy committed by your co-conspirators.

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: The Pinkerton liability point is important, and what it does is it sweeps in actions that you don't do because they're of co-conspirators, but it doesn't change the nature of what's the -- the inchoate offense. The reason the offense continues is because it's not an agreement to just say we're coming to an agreement. It's an agreement with the specific intent to do more, to commit this further crime.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. Let me put --

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: That's the same thing that we have here.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. One other question. Has the statute of limitations on this offense expired?

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: I actually --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: If you don't know. I was just wondering if we could be --

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: I actually don't know. If you're asking whether we would retry him --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: -- you know, that decision has not been made, but I'll give you one fact that may counsel against it, which is he was sentenced to 42 months of imprisonment for all six of his offenses, including this. There was a sentencing error that the Ninth Circuit remanded for. And so, even though upholding all the offenses, it went back for resentencing, and by that time, he'd already served all 42 months, including the sentence for this crime.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: This one, okay. Thanks.

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: So, in terms of practical retrial, that would weigh as a factor that we would consider.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Jackson?

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: In the case of U.S. versus Rodriguez-Moreno in 1999, at Footnote 2, we noted that "the government argues that venue may also be permissibly based upon the effects of a defendant's conduct in a district other than the one in which the defendant performs the acts constituting the offense."

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: And we went on to say, "we express no opinion as to whether the government's assertion is correct."

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: So here today, do you at least concede that you're asking us for a new rule?

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: We don't think it's new because we think it's rooted in cases like --

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: No, but the Court reserved it in this prior case and said we express no opinion as to it. So today you're asking us to apply or to adopt the rule that you previously offered and that we expressed no opinion on, is that correct?

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: Yes. But just because Rodriguez-Moreno didn't express an opinion on it doesn't mean that there aren't prior cases that speak to the issue, like Lamar, like Ford, which in the extraterritorial context, which the question's basically the same, which is --

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: Right, but as --

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: -- did the offense occur in the jurisdiction --

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: -- as of 19- -- as of 1999, the Court had not understood itself to have answered that question and the government was offering it and we said we're expressing no opinion, correct?

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: No. I think that's over-reading it. Expressing no opinion is different from saying we haven't resolved the question.

turns.name: HOWELL JACKSON
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: It's just not resolving it there.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: ANTHONY A. YANG
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Mr. Loss-Eaton, rebuttal? MR. LOSS-EATON: So I think my friend really hung his hat on the analogy to conspiracy. His silver bullet in his view is that you can be tried anywhere an overt act is undertaken further if it's a -- a conspiracy, even for conspiracies where an overt act is not an element. And the problem with that view, in addition to the points that various members of the Court have made about conspiracy being a unique, ongoing collective enterprise, is that if you look at the cases in which the Court adopted that rule for conspiracy, it did not think about it at all in the terms that my friend is describing. In Hyde in 1912, the Court first allowed venue for conspiracy where an overt act was committed even though the conspiracy was formed elsewhere, and, there, an overt act was an element. But the Court explained the integral role of the overt act in giving the conspiracy life and continuing it forward, and it quoted a New York case from the 1830s that said, wherever the conspirators act, there, they renew or, perhaps to speak more properly, they continue their agreement. So, in allowing venue based on overt acts, the Court conceptualized the overt act as itself being the agreement in a real sense because an agreement without any overt acts really doesn't matter. It doesn't do anything. And so then, in Trenton Pottery and Socony-Vacuum, which are the two cases that the Court was summarizing in Whitfield, which the government cites so many times in its briefs, the Court then applied Hyde to conspiracies where an overt act was not an element, essentially with no real analysis beyond saying, yes, that's still true here. In Socony-Vacuum, the Court said that the overt acts supplied part of the continuous cooperation necessary to keep the conspiracy alive. So I think it's very clear from those opinions that the Court conceptualized of an overt act, whether required as a standalone element or not, as being part of the actus reus of the conspiracy itself. And it was on that basis that the Court allowed venue. And, of course, here, that is not true for the effects that the government is vaguely asserting happened in San Francisco. Beyond that, my friend returned a few times to the analogy of someone aiming a gun from Seattle at San Francisco and pulling the trigger. To the extent Mr. Abouammo was aiming anything at anybody, it was at the people who were sitting in his living room in Seattle. Whether the investigation was ongoing in San Francisco or anywhere else I suspect was probably not top of mind. There were federal agents sitting in his living room in Seattle and he wanted them to go away and so, to that extent, I think, Justice Barrett, it is the same as the AirDrop analogy or printing out the paper and handing it to them. His conduct was in Seattle and it was directed at people who were in Seattle. Nor is conspiracy an inchoate offense in any other way. The literal dictionary definition of an inchoate offense is solicitation, attempt, conspiracy. This is none of those. This is not an attempt offense because it applies only to a specific kind of conduct and not to a substantial step of whatever type. And it's not an attempt offense because it doesn't incorporate any objective offense whose elements you would have to think about. And I do think that distinguishes a lot of the hypotheticals that the Court asked about. Justice Alito, the -- the hypothetical that you raised about fraud that is attempted to be committed by transmitting a document. I think, under most of the fraud statutes, and, certainly, once you start thinking about attempt, there's no problem with venue there on our theory as long as that transmission is part of the actus reus that you're talking about. And for an attempted fraud, it would be because that would be a substantial step. It might even be fraud itself depending on what the effects were. So I don't think those kinds of situations are implicated. I do also want to emphasize, of course, all we're talking about here is where this crime can be tried. There's a temptation sometimes when you're talking about these issues to think that we're trying to immunize the conduct from prosecution. That is not what's going on here. All we're talking about is the appropriate place of trial, and that is where the offense was committed. A lot of the Court's questions, I think, underscore the problems with the government's rule. I think, you know, fully 80 percent of the questions that I heard were just trying to figure out what the government's rule is, how it works, and what relationship, if any, it has to the statutory text. And I think Mr. Yang's answers underscore that it is deeply unclear, the relationship to the actual statutory text and elements is extremely attenuated, and, as you were getting at, Justice Sotomayor, it's very unclear how it would actually work. The facts that the government wants you to rely on to uphold Mr. Abouammo's conviction here were not charged in the indictment and they were never found by the jury. On what basis then can you uphold the conviction even on the government's theory? And even if you adopt that theory for future cases, does that mean the government now has to charge those facts in the indictment and prove them at trial? It is a much simpler approach that will do no violence to the law simply to say what the Court has always said: We are looking to where you committed the essential conduct elements of the offense, and, here, all of the essential conduct elements were committed in Seattle.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
